# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. A Comedy Movie That Never Gets Old?

**Score:** 98
**Body:** What comedy movie never fails to make you laugh, no matter how many times you’ve seen it? Share your go-to pick! Always cracks up!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Groundhog Day!
2. Big Lebowski
3. Galaxy Quest
4. The Princess Bride
5. Watched Young Frankenstein last night, still makes me laugh. Blazing Saddles today
6. Office Space. Especially if you work in an office. Still to this day people refer to it all the time.
7. Monty Python And The Holy Grail
8. Raising Arizona
9. *Airplane*!
10. All of Me

Tremors

So I Married an Axw Murderer

Man with Two Brains

## 2. Late night sci-fi greats, like Soldier (1998)

**Score:** 15
**Body:** I remember staying up late playing World of Warcraft as a teenager and seeing this movie called Soldier (1998) at like 1 in the morning on the sci fi channel. Movie was awesome, really captured my attention with the setting and I loved the whole vibe. 

Anyway, what are some more sci-fi late night greats? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Starship Troopers
2. Universal Soldier (1992) - hard to get more badass than Jean-Claude Van Damme and Dolph Lundgren
  - More Dolph Lundgren: I Come in Peace, aka Dark Angel.
3. Outland
4. Event Horizon (1997)
5. Cherry 2000

Spacehunter: Adventures In The Forbidden Zone 

Metalstorm: The Destruction Of Jared-Syn
6. Blade Runner (1982)
7. Gattaca ,
Screamers,
Dark City,
Johnny Mnemonic
8. Virtuosity
9. Hardware. 

Virus.

## 3. Frantic Disturbing Psychological Horror

**Score:** 7
**Body:** I'm bored, and in the mood to watch one of those fucked up movies.

Can you guys recommend me some fucked up disturbing psychological horror films?

But, something frantic, fast, disorienting and disturbing, not only plot wise but even regarding the sound and the camera motion.

For reference, something heavy like Martyrs, with the disorienting frantic Gaspar Noé style, if you know what I mean.

Thanks. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mother! (2017) - starts off slow but then gets frantic
2. Check out Climax if you haven't already - it's literally Gaspar Noé and hits all those boxes perfectly. The whole thing is just a descent into madness with insane camera work and sound design

  
Also maybe try Mandy or Possession (1981), both are absolutely unhinged in the best way
3. Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas!

In addition to the disorienting cinematography, there isn't much of a plot, which adds to the disorientation. 

Some may argue that it's not a true psychological horror. The MCs reach some pretty disturbing depths of depravity plus there's a kidnapping so I'd say it counts.

It's not very dark tho. In fact, it's quite colorful.
4. Mads
5. Idk if this will be paced fast enough for you, but it is psychological horror--

*10 Cloverfield Lane (2016)*
6. Stop whatever you're doing and watch [**Possessor**](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/435615-possessor#play=2kV3IJcFRX8) fucking immediately.
7. I take it you’ve seen 28 Years Later?
  - not yet, actually
8. Mother!

Irreversible.

Requiem for a Dream.

Not horror per se but may be what you are looking for.
9. ok so you want your brain scrambled like an egg while also being emotionally destroyed, respect the commitment.

hereditary is the obvious one but you probably already know it. raw 2016) hits different with the sound design alone making you feel sick. if you really want the gaspar noé chaos energy, irreversible is literally him doing exactly that but it's... a lot. come true has that disorienting hypnotic nightmare vibe, host (2020) on zoom calls shouldn't work but it's genuinely unsettling as hell.

warning: some of these will make you question your life choices but that's kind of the point right?

## 4. People Howling

**Score:** 9
**Body:** Kind of a strange ask but any movie/shows with people howling, any context if fine so far my list consists of: 

\-Pluribus

\-Yellowjackets 

\-Where the Wild Things Are 

 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Kim Catral in Porkys . I think it’s her character that howls.
  - They call her Lassie.
2. **Death To Smoochy**

Don't look it up. Just watch it.
  - not enough people mention this movie

absolutely bonkers film
3. Rutger Hauer/Roy Batty does some howling at the end of Blade Runner
4. Wolfwalkers. They howl for like the entire movie, but it's a genuinely good film too.
5. THE HOWLING of course
6. There is an episode of The Twilight Zone called “The Howling Man.”
7. Theres an episode of Fraiser where a girl he's dating howls at the moon. Then they both howl.
8. The Texas Chain Saw Massacre dinner scene. ✌️🤪

## 5. Surviving the Game (1994)

**Score:** 6
**Body:** Ok, so I’ve always been a fan of these kinds of movies ever since I read the OG Most Dangerous Game as a kid. There are definitely a lot of these movies out there like Hard Target and Running Man to an extent, but this one definitely is much better than it got credit for. 

Great scenery and honestly great cast with Rutger Hauer, Gary Busey, F. Murray Abraham, and Ice T before SVU. (Busey playing his usual crazy self gives an awesome monologue that I’ll never forget)

Not an Oscar candidate by any stretch of the phrase but actually really fucking entertaining.  Definitely check it out if you have 90 minutes and you want a good little action flick. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. We owe Gary Busey a lot more for the "Prince Henry Stout" monologue. The script had only a few lines. He was of the opinion that it wasn't enough for the audience to understand his reason for being part of the 'game'. So, he had approached Dickerson, and had asked if he could improvise.   
It does go toe-to-toe in terms of awesomeness with Boril Karloff's "The Appointment in Samarra" in *Targets* (1968), doesn't it?
  - That makes it so much better knowing it was improvised. I posted this as I’m currently watching it and I just got to the cave seen with Mcginley, and damn honestly not bad acting chops from either of them there either for another scene with dialogue
2. Check out Turkey Shoot (1982) if you haven't. Don't get me wrong, it's fucking terrible, but for some reason it has a random werewolf in it and if that's not something then I don't know how to finish this sentence or make a point. Also, Quentin Tarantino wants you to watch Battle Royale (2000).
3. Damn it was having a good run so far but I just lost The Game.
  - I had a run for a solid 3 years, and somehow in 2026, I've lost the game 6 times already.
4. You should check out 'The Naked Prey' (1965) if you haven't seen it.
  - Oh yeah definitely have. Super over the top but definitely one of the first of these kinds of films in the mainstream… like honestly.. how would some dude escape from like 7 African hunters in Africa just because you went to college. But entertaining, nonetheless
    - Cool. Yeah, I guess a lot of films suffer from that. If I recall correctly, Van Damme jumps up and balances standing up right on a motorcycle while firing a pistol in 'Hard Target' (1993) too. I loved that scene as a kid.
5. Hard Target with Jean Claude Van Damme and directed by John Woo is a great addition to that style of film.
6. ICE-T had a good run in the early 90s

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. A hilarious moment in an otherwise serious movie. I'll start the combat sequence between hal and dauphin of france.{The king}

**Score:** 650
**Body:** Context- ((2nd image))The Dauphin of France talked big throughout the film, but when he finally faced Hal one-on-one, he couldn’t even stand and kept slipping in the mud.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is a very underrated movie. I loved the twist at the end.
  - I hated that he wasn’t even a dolphin
    - They did that on porpoise
2. In Heat when Al Pacino says "she has a GREAT ASS and you've got your head ALL THE WAY UP IT!"

It's an incredible movie with some of the best acting DeNiro and Pacino have ever done, but his face and delivery during that scene are so ridiculous.
  - He was on all the cocaine during that movie.
3. The scene in Braveheart where I learned the meaning of the word defenestration.  It is such a darkly humorous scene that just comes out of nowhere.  Prince Edward's close friend and advisor deigns to give Edward "Longshanks" some advice and Edward leads him to the window as if taking him seriously and then immediately throws him out the window and 100 foot drop.  You laugh, sometimes its a shocking laugh, or a nervous laugh, but it is definitely darkly humorous.
  - The way he casually slaps his son for trying to murder him and then says he can’t send him to negotiate since that will just encourage an invasion adds to the dark humor. Meanwhile said son is grieving his dead bf.
    - “Not my gentle son”.
4. I really liked this film. Robert Patterson is an excellent actor obviously, but I thought Timothee Chalamet was really good
5. “Great big balls….but a tiiinnnnyyy little cock”

## 2. Bill Murray - BIG ERN

**Score:** 330
**Body:** Bill Murray is obviously known as a great actor with his, more often than not, cheeky, charming characters with witty ways.

Having watched Kingpin again recently, I paid very close attention to Murrays performance in the movie, and my God, it's amazingly done. It's one of those roles and actors that were just absolutely made for each other, and it shows. I don't know if Bill Murray had any input into the way Ernie McCrakens character would develop, but it oozes Murray himself, and you could tell he loved being him. Harrelson is amazing in this movie too.

Thoughts on the movie and Big Ern?

Any other "fun" roles from other movies where you could see how much the actor was enjoying performing it?

Thanks

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “Hey. Not you. Hey.”
  - "Do me a favour and go and finish that outside"
    - Supplemental? Income?
  - Best line in the show, also “hey everybody there’s a shit cloud coming!!!”
2. Big Ern feels like Murray turning his charm up to 11 and using it for pure evil. Every line, every smirk, you can tell he’s having the time of his life being an absolute menace. Total lightning-in-a-bottle casting.

Brad Pitt in Burn After Reading and Leo in Once Upon a Time in Hollywood give me the same vibe. When actors are clearly having fun, it elevates everything.
3. Fun fact: Bill Murray made those three strikes in one take. Everyone's reaction in that scene was genuine.
4. 1979 Waitress: Tanqueray and Tab.


Ernie McCracken: Keep 'em comin', sweets, I got a long drive. Do me a favor, will you? Would you mind washing off that perfume before you come back to our table?
5. I think the entire cast of the Soderberg Oceans Eleven were having a blast.
6. "That's not case at all, the woman's a stone faced liar. Let's not even talk about it, I pulled out really early on that one."
7. "HI, not you. You, hi."

## 3. What you think about this one?

**Score:** 153

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Loved it! Interesting idea, good execution and a touching ending.
2. Very well made movie
  - unlike that poster - sheesh
3. Enjoyed it. Not exactly original but entertaining and well acted. Similar in theme to the earlier Deja Vu (2006), also a good film.
4. I enjoyed it.
5. I really liked it, but I like any time loop movie
  - I'm a whore for any time loop movie or book (or pamphlet)
6. Bit heartbreaking at the end, quite frankly
7. Very well made, really enjoyed it. It’s definitely a go to again and again movie
8. It's a good movie with an interesting premise. Best part is when we see how he really looks like physically, which I reacted similarly with how RoboCop was revealed (with his armor an old prosthetic removed, revealing just his head and his vital organs)

## 4. Just rewatched Foxcatcher. Steve Carell really proved his range in this role. I wish we saw him take on more parts like this.

**Score:** 140

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's not the same role, but he already has other roles to showcase his acting range.

Two come to mind: The Big Short and Cafe Society, to name just a couple.
  - They were good roles, but not as good as Foxcatcher which got him Oscar nominated. He should have won over Eddie Redmayne imo.
  - Different role but he also plays a “jerk” character in “The way, way back”. I really enjoy is acting but I actually caught myself thinking he was a legitimate jerk before realizing it’s just that character. 

I’d say his humor alone also has a unique range. Dry at times and others just silly.
  - Little miss sunshine was a great turn too
2. God, what an amazing horrific film. I never want to see it ever again.
3. Incredible film with incredible performances all around
4. Like robin Williams in one hour photo
5. Ruffalo was amazing in this as well. That walk he had.
6. If you like this movie you should see the documentaries, there are 2 that I know of, one is on Netflix.
7. Great film. Kinda glosses over some facts about what happened at Foxcatcher but Steve Carrell was scary good

## 5. Just finished watching Peaky Blinders. Is the show Fairly Rated or Overrated ?

**Score:** 73

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I liked it, but the plot armour the family had was on a whole other level. Like by rights Luca Changretta  would have killed the whole family before revealing himself. Him being a stranger in the UK wouldn't have stopped it, he would have hired all the right people to see it through.
  - The whole "out of respect, I'm telling you that I intend to kill you" was the most un-mafia thing ever. Those guys flourished for decades because you never saw them coming.
2. One of those shows where the style actually outweighs some of the substance.

Some of the writing is kinda corny and/or formulaic. But then they do one of those shots of them walking in slow motion with the Black Keys or something playing in the background and it's *awesome*.
  - 100% style and atmosphere. 

My single friend says she swipes left on any profile that mentions Peaky Blinders, SoA and Godfather. I don't blame her.
3. Slightly overrated in my opinion, but still a good show. I watched it after the show Warrior, and maybe that skewed my judgement.
  - This is my take as well. Great performances, fascinating characters, and often visually stunning, but the show is more style over substance. Every season felt like it hit the same beats but upped the stakes.
  - Goddamn shame Warrior never got to finish
4. R.I.P Aunt Polly
5. Honestly, s1-2 were amazing but I grew bored of it
6. If you like something, the ratings don't matter.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. "Cries and Whispers"

**Score:** 11
**Body:** I've been sort of on this Bergman kick I guess and so I'm watching more of his films after Persona -- I'd seen the Seventh Seal and Wild Strawberries a long time ago and liked them, but Persona really shook me up in a way that I wasn't prepared for, and I feel like "Cries and Whispers" is doing that to me now, I'm only in the first third so far but I already find it quite beautiful in an extremely icy way. I don't know that I like a lot of the characters so much as I feel that they are very realistic to human nature. I'm sort of excited to work my way through Bergman's filmography because I didn't really recognize that dimension in his work before, the sort of darkness that is also tempered by a deep curiosity about human nature. It's kind of like confronting the fear of death -- there is no way to do so that is not completely upsetting, there's not even a way of doing so that resolves the fear. But there is a way that confronting that fear lessens the grip that the fear has over us. And I think that's worth doing.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is very exciting. I fell in love with him when I got the Criterion Channel five years ago. Watched 25 or so over a year and just revisited about a dozen in December for the first time since. He’s the greatest. C&W was very painful for me the first time around but I found a lot more of the joy in it this time. 

Here’s my ranking if you want to know where to go next, though there aren’t many bad options: https://boxd.it/atobG

I still most need to watched the film versions of Scenes… an F&A which I’ll get to soon. 

Savor these first watched. Turn off the lights and put your phone in another room. 
2. My first Bergman, and still close to being my favorite. Top five, surely. That almost suffocating red enveloping everything, flecked with the whites and blacks of clothing; the command with which the story is told, with its masterful dissolves and gracefully integrated flashbacks...IB wrote in *Images* that in this film and *Persona*, he felt he had gone as far as it was possible for him to go in using the resources of cinema to tell stories that could not be told in another medium. (However, after he died, there was that Van Hove stage adaptation of C&W. I have not seen it.) 

Harriet Andersson's portrayal of physical suffering is as powerful as her portrayal of emotional/mental suffering had been a decade earlier in *Through a Glass Darkly*. I've heard a lot of actors scream in pain in movies, TV shows and plays. I cannot think of another who made me have to remind myself that the actor was okay, was not really suffering. (This was, in fact, a very happy and harmonious set, with a lot of laughter and girl talk.)
  - It's very interesting that he compares this to Persona. Watching both of these films it feels to me the same way -- I don't know how much farther you can go really. I'm not super familiar with his whole filmography so I'm curious to see other of his movies at this level of expression. If you were to name your top 5 films by him what would they be?
    - I'll give you ten! 

*Fanny and Alexander* (miniseries version preferred; shorter theatrical cut will do in a pinch) 

*Cries and Whispers* 

*Wild Strawberries* 

*Persona* 

*The Seventh Seal* 

*Shame* 

*Winter Light*

*The Magician* 

*The Silence* 

*From the Life of the Marionettes* 


I can almost hear other aficionados asking, "Where's *Smiles of a Summer Night*? Where's *Autumn Sonata*? Where's *Hour of the Wolf*?" The above were just the ten I felt most strongly about the last time I watched a number of his films close together. There are about 30 that are at least very good. I even think *The Serpent's Egg* (a big-budget thriller in English with an American leading man, considered a failure when new) is better than its reputation. 

Happy exploring.

## 2. Mildred Pierce / Parent-Child / Justice

**Score:** 0
**Body:** # Is MILDRED PIERCE really the first example of a parent trying to protect their child from justice? James M. Cain published the novel in 1945, seems like there'd be earlier [versions of this trope](https://letterboxd.com/9413/list/parents-protecting-children-from-punishment/), even in the bible or myths even, hmph always interested in ur-examples and antecedents etc assuming someone knows more than me about this particular topic.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)
